episodes.title: Frontier AI Lab Competition — July 8, 2026
episodes.summary: OpenAI's GPT-5.6 family (Sol, Terra, Luna) re-prices frontier intelligence as it self-cannibalizes on cost; Google scraps and rebuilds Gemini 3.5 Pro for a July 17 launch; Anthropic's 19-day Fable 5 export-control shutdown sets a dual-use regulatory template; and Chinese open weights keep setting the price floor beneath a $725B capex arms race.

episodes.title: Capital Markets Cross-Asset Radar — July 8, 2026
episodes.summary: A hawkish Fed and a fresh Iran oil shock pull the same way: the June FOMC minutes show officials floating rate hikes with PCE revised to 3.6%, the 30-year yield tops 5%, Brent jumps past $78, and equities shrug while Bitcoin and the 60/40 bear the strain.

episodes.title: Digital Money, Crypto, and Market Structure — July 8, 2026
episodes.summary: The real institutionalization story is the digital-dollar stack — regulated stablecoins, $7B in tokenized Treasuries, bank-chartered issuers, and Coinbase's yield-arbitrage — all racing the GENIUS Act's July 18 rule deadline, even as Bitcoin trades like leveraged Nasdaq and catches no haven bid during the Iran strikes.

episodes.title: Strategic Power, Energy, and Institutional Change — July 8, 2026
episodes.summary: Energy became the base layer of power in real time this week: US strikes on Iran put the Strait of Hormuz back in play and spike oil, NATO's Ankara summit turns rearmament into $50B+ of procurement, and America's AI buildout is throttled not by chips but by transformers, switchgear, and an interconnect queue that tariffs are taxing.

episodes.title: Digital Money, Crypto, and Market Structure — July 9, 2026
episodes.summary: The news is that the SEC is preparing to propose its first major crypto rule as soon as this month — an initiative the agency is calling "Regulation Crypto." The substance is what matters: it would create temporary exemptions from securities registration for developers first issuing crypto investment contracts, allow…

episodes.title: Strategic Power, Energy, and Institutional Change — July 9, 2026
episodes.summary: Start with the number that should reframe how you think about data-center economics. In PJM — the grid operator covering thirteen states and the largest power market in the country — the capacity auction for the 2026-2027 delivery year cleared at 329.17 dollars per megawatt-day, across all zones, a record.

episodes.title: Frontier AI Lab Competition — July 9, 2026
episodes.summary: Today is the day OpenAI actually opens the door. GPT-5.6 — the Sol, Terra, and Luna family that was announced back on June 26th and has been sitting in a tightly gated preview — expands its rollout today, and with the expansion we finally have the thing that was missing at announcement: numbers.

episodes.title: Capital Markets Cross-Asset Radar — July 9, 2026
episodes.summary: Here's the concrete evidence. The implied probability of a Fed rate hike at the September meeting jumped to roughly 70 percent, up from 58 percent just the prior session. That's a twelve-point move in a day, and it's not about term premium anymore.

episodes.title: Semiconductors & Compute Infrastructure — July 9, 2026
episodes.summary: Begin with NVIDIA, because it frames everything. Its most recent quarter was 81.6 billion dollars in revenue, with data-center revenue up 92 percent year over year to 75.2 billion. And through June, it began ramping its next-generation Vera Rubin platform into production.

episodes.title: AI Products & Applications — July 9, 2026
episodes.summary: The most telling product development this week isn't a new coding agent — it's a product whose entire job is to manage the coding agents you already have. Journi launched DevOS, a platform to measure, govern, and optimize AI-assisted software development across tools like Claude Code and Cursor.

episodes.title: Enterprise Technology & Software — July 9, 2026
episodes.summary: The defining question in enterprise software right now is brutally simple: does AI eat SaaS, or does it feed it? For the past several months the market has been trading on the fear that autonomous agents make seat-based software obsolete — why pay for a thousand licenses when an agent does the work?
